justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California wage statement/AB5, Germany works council, Indonesia contractor model. (2) Germany works council approval identified as hard deployment gate (BR-4, AC-3). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified (BR-7) with employee vs contractor implications. (4) Formal escalations raised for legal review with structured options. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well below 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — California Labor Code §226 and §351 cited, German BetrVG §87(1)(6) cited, Indonesia correctly identified as no legal mandate
Works council approval modeled as a hard deployment gate with feature flag (works_council_approval flag), not just a mention
Driver classification difference (employee vs contractor) identified as invariant BR-7 with clear data model implications
Subtle trap surfaced: California compliance cannot be removed globally even if Indonesia doesn't require it (implicit in BR-7 and jurisdiction-specific AC structure)
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as necessary — 'three distinct compliance regimes behind one UI'
Edge case for works council revocation (disabling feature while preserving historical data) shows deep understanding
Confidence at 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas and explicit assumptions
Escalations are structured with options and recommendations, not just flags — actionable for stakeholders

grader_results.detail: field escalations[*].type equals check failed: expected 'ambiguity', got values: 'missing_context'
